items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m plagued by vague doubts again…
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder, the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Largely succeeds at the key comedic effect: mock-heroic phrasing (“plagued by vague doubts”) applied to petty theft. Crucially, it correctly avoids the wrong possessive reading (“Shpak has… the ambassador has…”) and instead conveys loss/missing items, matching the reference note. Minor nuance: “haunt me” is slightly more elevated/idiomatic than “I’m plagued by,” but both preserve the theatrical register.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Tsar—very nice to meet you. Tsar. Very nice. Tsar. Very nice to meet you—hello, Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Captures the repetitive, awkwardly polite Soviet-style greeting and the comic insistence on “tsar” as a social label. However, it slightly blurs the intended gag that Bunsha is effectively introducing himself as “the Tsar” (a role/title as identity) rather than addressing the other person as “Tsar.” The reference’s “I’m the Tsar” / “the Tsar” makes the self-introduction aspect clearer. Current version still works in context (he is speaking to the tsarina/others), but the comedic self-branding is weakened.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the abrupt authoritarian register and comic escalation.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I'm in mourning.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Keeps the archaic/high-register dismissal and the comic mismatch with the modern setting. However, “in mourning” implies bereavement/ritual mourning rather than generalized melancholy (“в печали”). That shifts meaning and slightly changes the tone of the tsar’s lofty lament into something more specific than intended.

items.translation:
They’ve walled me in!
They’ve walled me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Preserves the core misunderstanding (mechanical entrapment read as demonic masonry) and keeps “demons” as the agent. Slight punctuation/intonation difference (exclamation vs ellipsis) but comedic sense remains intact.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is correct and keeps the religious attribution. But the reference’s “Behold the power…” better matches the tsar’s elevated, proclamatory register and the mock-biblical flourish that heightens the joke in an atheist Soviet context. Current phrasing is a bit flat/modern-demonstrative (“That’s what… can do”), slightly reducing the comic grandiosity.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic directive to dance, but loses the specific performative/MC-like catchphrase quality. “Танцуют все!” is a set, showman’s announcement (“Everyone dances!” / “All dance!”) that frames the scene as a mass, compulsory, carnival-like moment. “Everybody dance!” reads more like a simple command and is less quotable/iconic; it also slightly narrows the meaning from “everyone is dancing” (announcement) to “you should dance” (imperative).

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat satisfaction at stumbling into a profitable situation. Slight loss of the colloquial self-congratulation (“I came in at just the right time / I picked a good moment to drop by”), but “What a lucky break!” still lands the intended opportunistic cheer and is idiomatic for subtitles.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas spread...
eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Matches the reference’s key creative decision: avoids calling the eggplant item “caviar,” using “spread” instead, preserving the Soviet scarcity joke and preventing confusion. Rhythm/ellipsis mimic the comic reveal well.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket… three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Preserves the escalating inventory and the comic trailing “three” after the suede jacket. “Domestic cigarette cases” is a good rendering of «отечественных». The only slight drawback is added framing (“Everything I earned… is gone:”) which is present in the broader scene but not essential to the meme line; still, it doesn’t harm the joke and keeps Shpak’s melodramatic self-pity.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Accurate and keeps the refrain. However, the exclamation marks push it toward emphatic scolding, while the reference analysis highlights a calm, melodic, resigned cadence that makes the joke (shared madness/psychiatric ward) land. Using ellipses (or softer punctuation) would better preserve the deadpan, sing-song fatalism.

summary.interpretation: 1.64 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.33 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
